title: The carbon and water cycles
examTip: Carbon cycle questions often ask you to name the processes that move carbon between stores (photosynthesis, respiration, combustion, decomposition). Water cycle questions focus on the processes — evaporation, transpiration, condensation, precipitation. Know which direction each process moves carbon or water, and be able to label a diagram of either cycle.
introTips: Carbon and water are constantly recycled — the same atoms have been cycling through living things for billions of years.
For the carbon cycle, learn which process does what: photosynthesis removes CO₂ from the air, respiration and combustion put it back.
The water cycle links to transpiration from your plant biology — water evaporates from leaves, contributing to the cycle.
intro: All matter on Earth is recycled — atoms pass continuously between living organisms, the atmosphere, the soil, and the oceans. The carbon cycle and water cycle are two of the most important nutrient cycles in ecology. Understanding them explains how burning fossil fuels disrupts the carbon balance, and how plants and weather interact to distribute water across the planet.

keyPoints.heading: The carbon cycle — processes and stores
keyPoints.content: Carbon is found in several stores: the atmosphere (as CO₂), living organisms (in organic molecules), the soil (in dead organic matter), and fossil fuels (oil, coal, gas). Carbon moves between stores via key processes. Photosynthesis removes CO₂ from the atmosphere and converts it to glucose (and other organic molecules) in plants. Respiration (by all living organisms) releases CO₂ back to the atmosphere. Combustion (burning fossil fuels and wood) releases stored carbon as CO₂. Decomposition by bacteria and fungi breaks down dead organic matter, releasing CO₂ through their respiration and returning minerals to the soil. Feeding transfers carbon from one organism to another through food chains.
writeBullets: Carbon stores: atmosphere (CO₂), organisms (organic molecules), soil, fossil fuels
Photosynthesis: removes CO₂ from atmosphere → stores carbon in organic molecules
Respiration: releases CO₂ back to atmosphere (all living organisms)
Combustion: burning releases stored carbon as CO₂
Decomposition: decomposers respire → release CO₂, return minerals to soil
Feeding: transfers carbon through food chains

keyTerms.def: A place where carbon is held for a period of time — atmosphere, organisms, soil, fossil fuels
Burning — releases carbon stored in fossil fuels or organic matter as CO₂ into the atmosphere
The breakdown of dead organic matter by decomposers (bacteria and fungi) — releases CO₂ and returns minerals to the soil

keyPoints.memTip: Carbon INTO atmosphere: respiration, combustion, decomposition. Carbon OUT OF atmosphere: photosynthesis. Three in, one out. The problem with fossil fuels is that we're adding carbon that was locked away for millions of years — photosynthesis can't balance it out fast enough.

keyPoints.heading: Human impact on the carbon cycle
keyPoints.content: For millions of years, the carbon cycle was roughly balanced — carbon released by respiration and decomposition was matched by carbon absorbed by photosynthesis. Human activities have disrupted this balance. Burning fossil fuels releases CO₂ that was locked underground for millions of years — adding it to the atmosphere faster than photosynthesis can remove it. Deforestation removes trees that would absorb CO₂ by photosynthesis, AND releases CO₂ when trees are burned or decomposed. The result is an increasing atmospheric CO₂ concentration, which is the primary driver of the greenhouse effect and climate change.
writeBullets: Carbon cycle was roughly balanced before human activity
Burning fossil fuels: releases ancient stored carbon as CO₂ faster than photosynthesis absorbs it
Deforestation: removes CO₂-absorbing trees; burning/decomposition of trees also releases CO₂
Result: increasing atmospheric CO₂ → enhanced greenhouse effect → climate change

keyTerms.def: The trapping of heat in Earth's atmosphere by greenhouse gases (e.g. CO₂, methane) — essential for life, but enhanced by human activity leading to global warming
The removal of trees on a large scale — reduces CO₂ absorption and often releases stored carbon

keyPoints.memTip: Balance disrupted by: Burning fossil fuels (adds ancient CO₂) + Deforestation (removes CO₂ absorbers + releases carbon). Both actions increase atmospheric CO₂. Link this to the greenhouse effect and climate change.

keyPoints.heading: The water cycle — evaporation, condensation and precipitation
keyPoints.content: Water moves continuously between the atmosphere, land, and living organisms. Key processes: Evaporation — water from oceans, rivers, and land surfaces evaporates into water vapour when heated by the sun. Transpiration — water evaporates from leaves of plants (through stomata) — this is a significant contributor to atmospheric water vapour. Condensation — as water vapour rises and cools, it condenses to form water droplets, creating clouds. Precipitation — when water droplets in clouds combine and become heavy enough, they fall as rain, sleet, or snow. Surface runoff and groundwater flow — water flows back to rivers and eventually to the sea, completing the cycle.
writeBullets: Evaporation: sun heats water → water vapour rises into atmosphere
Transpiration: water evaporates from plant leaves → contributes to atmospheric water vapour
Condensation: water vapour cools → forms water droplets → clouds form
Precipitation: water droplets combine → fall as rain/sleet/snow
Surface runoff: water flows back to rivers → sea → cycle repeats

keyTerms.def: The conversion of liquid water to water vapour at the water's surface, driven by heat energy from the sun
The evaporation of water from plant leaves through stomata — a major contributor to atmospheric water vapour
The conversion of water vapour back to liquid water as it cools — forms clouds
Water falling from clouds as rain, sleet, or snow

keyPoints.memTip: Water cycle: Evaporate up → Condense into clouds → Precipitate back down → Run off to sea → Evaporate again. ECPR — round and round. Transpiration is plants' contribution to evaporation — it's the water-cycle link to your plant biology.

0.question: Which process removes carbon dioxide from the atmosphere?
0.correctFeedback: ✅ Right — photosynthesis is the main process that removes CO₂ from the atmosphere, converting it into glucose and other organic molecules in plants.
0.wrongFeedback: Photosynthesis is the process that removes CO₂ from the atmosphere. Respiration, combustion, and decomposition all release CO₂ back into the atmosphere. Photosynthesis is the only major biological process that takes CO₂ out.

1.question: Why has atmospheric CO₂ increased significantly since the Industrial Revolution?
options: Organisms are respiring more due to larger global population
Burning fossil fuels releases ancient stored carbon, and deforestation removes CO₂-absorbing trees
Photosynthesis rates have decreased because plants are growing more slowly
Decomposition of soil has increased, releasing large amounts of CO₂
1.correctFeedback: ✅ Exactly — the two main drivers are burning fossil fuels (releasing ancient carbon) and deforestation (removing the trees that absorb CO₂).
1.wrongFeedback: The main causes of rising atmospheric CO₂ are: (1) burning fossil fuels — this releases carbon that was locked underground for millions of years, adding it to the atmosphere faster than photosynthesis can absorb it; and (2) deforestation — removing trees reduces the total photosynthesis capacity and releases stored carbon when they're burned or decompose.

commonMistakes: Saying photosynthesis releases CO₂ — it's the opposite. Photosynthesis uses CO₂ (removes it from the atmosphere). Respiration releases CO₂.
Forgetting decomposers in the carbon cycle — decomposers respire as they break down dead matter, releasing CO₂. This is an essential step in returning carbon to the atmosphere.
Confusing transpiration and evaporation — both result in water vapour entering the atmosphere, but transpiration is specifically water evaporating from plant leaves through stomata.
Saying the water cycle only involves oceans — transpiration from plants is a major source of atmospheric water vapour, especially in rainforests.
examTips: For carbon cycle questions: always name the process AND the direction of carbon movement. 'Photosynthesis removes CO₂ from the atmosphere and converts it to organic molecules' is a complete answer; just saying 'photosynthesis' is not.
If asked to label a carbon cycle diagram, identify the arrows by what's happening: CO₂ going into a plant = photosynthesis; CO₂ leaving organisms = respiration; CO₂ from burning = combustion.
For water cycle questions, sequence the four main stages: evaporation (and transpiration) → condensation → precipitation → surface runoff/groundwater → back to evaporation.
Human impact on carbon cycle: always give two reasons — fossil fuels AND deforestation. One reason rarely gets full marks for a 2-mark question.
revisionCardBullets: Carbon stores: atmosphere (CO₂), organisms, soil, fossil fuels
Photosynthesis: removes CO₂ from atmosphere → stores in organic molecules
Respiration + combustion + decomposition: release CO₂ back to atmosphere
Decomposers (bacteria/fungi): break down dead matter, release CO₂, return minerals
Human impact: burning fossil fuels + deforestation → increased atmospheric CO₂
Greenhouse effect: CO₂ traps heat → global warming / climate change
Water cycle: evaporation → condensation → precipitation → surface runoff
Transpiration: water evaporates from plant leaves → contributes to atmospheric water vapour
Condensation: water vapour cools → water droplets → clouds
Precipitation: water droplets too heavy → fall as rain/sleet/snow
